text: Livax single port splenectomy. These are my disclosures. This lady was in her late seventies with severe ITP relapsed on immunosuppression. She responded to corticosteroids and splenectomy was scheduled for two weeks later.

The surgeon enters through the applied medical gel port placed at the umbilicus, revealing the diaphragm with visible ridges. The spleen is grasped and drawn inferiorly, exposing a flat area of the diaphragm suitable for sealing. The Teleflex Minilab Clutch, measuring 2.3 millimeters, is visible.

The Livac device, a 56mm model, is inserted through the applied gel port, with tubing connected to the Livac connector and suction hose clamped at minus 500 millimeters of mercury. The tubing is positioned to the side, and the device is handled using small tabs on the ring.

The surgeon positions the suction ring against the spleen and a flat area of the diaphragm, activating suction to lift the spleen. As dissection progresses, the splenic hilar vessels become visible. The surgeon uses a Ligasure device to clear peripheral attachments and define the splenic vessels approaching the hilum.

The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the splenic vessels en masse, resulting in partial devascularization of the spleen. The tail of the pancreas becomes visible, extending into the splenic hilum. The surgeon proceeds to clear attachments at the superior pole of the spleen.
The surgeon focuses on clearing the attachments around the splenic hilum, preparing to staple them later. The splenic hilum is visible as the attachments are meticulously dissected.
The surgeon guides the Medtronic 60mm tan tri-stapler flush against the vessels entering the spleen, waits for 60 seconds before releasing the firing mechanism, completing the stapling process.

The surgeon uses a ligature to sever a small residual attachment, resulting in a levitating spleen. The suction hose is clamped, and the seal is broken, showing minimal marks on the diaphragm. The Livac retractor is gently removed, and a 15mm retrieval bag is inserted through the gel to scoop up the morselated spleen.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical site after the spleen has been removed piecemeal. The diaphragm shows minimal marking, which has nearly vanished. Ropivacaine 0.2% is sprayed over the area for postoperative pain management.
